The 2024 Nobel Peace Prize was awarded to Nihon Hidankyō "for its efforts to achieve a world free of nuclear weapons and for demonstrating through witness testimony that nuclear weapons must never be used again.""},"results":[{"url":"https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nihon_Hidankyo","title":"Nihon Hidankyo - Wikipedia","content":"Their activities included recording thousands of witness accounts, issuing resolutions and public appeals, and sending annual delegations to various international organisations, including the United Nations, to advocate for global nuclear disarmament.
The organisation was awarded the 2024 Nobel Peace Prize "for its efforts to achieve a world free of nuclear weapons and for demonstrating through witness testimony that nuclear weapons must never be used again".

Telephone interview, October 2024"."},{"url":"https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/2024_Nobel_Peace_Prize#:~:text=6%20External%20links-,Laureate,must%20never%20be%20used%20again.%22","title":"2024 Nobel Peace Prize - Wikipedia","content":"Co-chair of the Nihon Hidankyo, Toshiyuki Mimaki, expressed surprise at receiving the award, telling reporters that he thought "those fighting hard for peace in Gaza would deserve it." He then warned of the danger of nuclear weapons being used for terrorism, warning that if "Russia uses them against Ukraine, Israel against Gaza, it won't end there." He compared the situation of Gaza to "Japan 80 years ago."
(born 1959) — Norway — "for his work to achieve peace and democracy in Europe and the world in the last year." — Abid Raja
"Organisasjonen Nihon Hidankyō får Nobels fredspris for 2024" [The organization Nihon Hidankyō receives the Nobel Peace Prize for 2024]."},{"url":"https://www.nobelprize.org/prizes/peace/2024/press-release/","title":"The Nobel Peace Prize 2024 - Press release - NobelPrize.org","content":"The Norwegian Nobel Committee has decided to award the Nobel Peace Prize for 2024 to the Japanese organisation Nihon Hidankyo.
The Norwegian Nobel Committee wishes nevertheless to acknowledge one encouraging fact: No nuclear weapon has been used in war in nearly 80 years.
Next year will mark 80 years since two American atomic bombs killed an estimated 120 000 inhabitants of Hiroshima and Nagasaki.
In this way they are helping to maintain the nuclear taboo – a precondition of a peaceful future for humanity."},{"url":"https://www.nobelpeacecenter.org/en/news/a-nobel-peace-prize-for-a-world-free-from-nuclear-arms","title":"A Nobel Peace Prize for a world free from nuclear arms | Nobel Peace Center","content":"In the announcement, the committee stated that: “The decision to award the Nobel Peace Prize for 2024 to Nihon Hidankyo is securely anchored in Alfred Nobel’s will.
This year’s prize joins a distinguished list of Peace Prizes that the Committee has previously awarded to champions of nuclear disarmament and arms control.
“This prize is part of a longstanding tradition of Nobel Peace Prizes awarded for efforts toward nuclear disarmament, but it is also a prize to civil society.
As the Chairman of the Norwegian Nobel Committee stated in his announcement, the threat of nuclear weapon use looms large in today's ongoing conflicts.
An educational program for school students is also being developed, available both in Norway and internationally, as well as through NRK School on Monday morning."},{"url":"https://www.nobelpeaceprize.org/","title":"Nobel Peace Prize - Home","content":"The Nobel Peace Prize is awarded on the day of Alfred Nobel’s death, the 10th of December, a time-honored tradition since 1901."}]}

The Norwegian Nobel Committee has awarded the Nobel Peace Prize for 2024 to the Japanese organisation Nihon Hidankyo. The Norwegian Nobel Committee has awarded the Nobel Peace Prize for 2024 to Nihon Hidankyo for its efforts to achieve a world free of nuclear weapons and for demonstrating through witness testimony that nuclear weapons must never be used again. In the announcement, the committee stated that: “The decision to award the Nobel Peace Prize for 2024 to Nihon Hidankyo is securely anchored in Alfred Nobel’s will. The Peace Prize to Nihon Hidankyo emphasizes the vital importance of individuals engaging in conversations about the devastating impacts of war and the use of weapons on civilian populations, says Kjersti Fløgstad, Director of the Nobel Peace Center, about the Nobel Peace Prize 2024.","score":0.79883134,"raw_content":null},{"url":"https://www.npr.org/2024/10/11/nx-s1-5148728/2024-nobel-peace-prize","title":"2024 Nobel Peace Prize goes to Japanese atomic bomb survivors' group : NPR","content":"**2024 Nobel Peace Prize goes to Japanese atomic bomb survivors' group** **The Japanese grassroots group Nihon Hidankyo, an organization of World War II atomic bombing survivors, has won the 2024 Nobel Peace Prize for its efforts to create a world free of nuclear weapons.**. Toshiyuki Mimaki, the co-chair of Nihon Hidankyo, at a press conference after the group was awarded the 2024 Nobel Peace Prize, in Hiroshima, Japan, on Friday. Toshiyuki Mimaki, the co-chair of Nihon Hidankyo, at a press conference after the group was awarded the 2024 Nobel Peace Prize, in Hiroshima Friday. A Japanese anti-nuclear weapons group made up of survivors of the atomic bombings in Japan during World War II has won the 2024 Nobel Peace Prize. The Norwegian Nobel Committee said it has awarded the Japanese organization Nihon Hidankyo \"for its efforts to achieve a world free of nuclear weapons and for demonstrating through witness testimony that nuclear weapons must never be used again,\" sending a message to countries that are considering acquiring or threatening to use them.","score":0.780947,"raw_content":null},{"url":"https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=CJuzfyfsPSk","title":"Nihon Hidankyo announced as Nobel Peace Prize Winner ...","content":"Nihon Hidankyo, a Japanese group of atomic bomb survivors, has won the 2024 Nobel Peace Prize. for its efforts to rid the world of nuclear","score":0.7185356,"raw_content":null},{"url":"https://www.nobelprize.org/prizes/peace/2024/press-release","title":"The Nobel Peace Prize 2024 - Press release - NobelPrize.org","content":"## Nobel Peace Prize 2024. The Nobel Peace Prize logo. The Norwegian Nobel Committee has decided to award the Nobel Peace Prize for 2024 to the Japanese organisation Nihon Hidankyo. This grassroots movement of atomic bomb survivors from Hiroshima and Nagasaki, also known as Hibakusha, is receiving the Peace Prize for its efforts to achieve a world free of nuclear weapons and for demonstrating through witness testimony that nuclear weapons must never be used again. In awarding this year’s Nobel Peace Prize to Nihon Hidankyo, the Norwegian Nobel Committee wishes to honour all survivors who, despite physical suffering and painful memories, have chosen to use their costly experience to cultivate hope and engagement for peace. The decision to award the Nobel Peace Prize for 2024 to Nihon Hidankyo is securely anchored in Alfred Nobel’s will. The Nobel Peace Prize for 2024 fulfils Alfred Nobel’s desire to recognise efforts of the greatest benefit to humankind.","score":0.70531887,"raw_content":null},{"url":"https://www.pbs.org/newshour/world/watch-live-the-winner-of-the-2024-nobel-peace-prize-is","title":"WATCH: Group of Japanese survivors of the U.S. atomic bombings wins 2024 Nobel Peace Prize | PBS News","content":"# WATCH: Group of Japanese survivors of the U.S. atomic bombings wins 2024 Nobel Peace Prize. The Nobel Peace Prize was awarded Friday to Nihon Hidankyo, a Japanese organization of survivors of the U.S. atomic bombings of Hiroshima and Nagasaki, for its activism against nuclear weapons. Jørgen Watne Frydnes, chair of the Norwegian Nobel Committee, said the award was made as the \"taboo against the use of nuclear weapons is under pressure.\". Shigemitsu Tanaka, a member of Japan Confederation of A- and H-Bomb Sufferers Organizations (Nihon Hidankyo), who survived the 1945 atomic bombing of Nagasaki, cries after the 2024 Nobel Peace Prize winner was announced in Nagasaki, Japan, on Oct. 11, 2024. \"It is very clear that threats of using nuclear weapons are putting pressure on the important international norm, the taboo of using nuclear weapons,\" Watne Frydnes said in response to a question on whether the rhetoric from Russia surrounding nuclear weapons in its invasion of Ukraine had influenced this year's decision.","score":0.68496037,"raw_content":null}],"response_time":1.71,"request_id":"55354fca-5d26-4c68-b720-cbde3c3ac98b"}
